description: This scatter plot illustrates the relationship between total elongation (TE, %) and ultimate tensile strength (UTS, MPa) for Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels, tested using uniform specimen geometry conforming to ASTM E-8 standard (50 mm × 12.5 mm × 1 mm). The tensile data represent solution-treated and water-quenched conditions compiled from multiple studies. The graph categorizes alloys into distinct phase-based groups: δ single phase (ferritic), δ-based duplex, γ single phase (labeled as Mn-TWIP), γ-based duplex, and γ+(δ)+coarse κ (including martensitic MS), alongside conventional automotive steels (IF, Mild, IF-HS, BH, CMn, TRIP, DP/CP) for comparison. Data points reveal that γ single-phase austenitic steels achieve superior strength-ductility combinations (UTS ~800-1400 MPa with TE ~30-100%), while δ-based duplex structures exhibit moderate properties (UTS ~400-900 MPa, TE ~20-70%). Alloys containing coarse κ-carbides or martensite (MS) show high strength but limited ductility (TE <10%). Diagonal lines indicate constant UTS×TE products (10-70 GPa%), demonstrating that Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys, particularly austenitic TWIP steels, outperform conventional high-strength steels in strength-ductility balance.
